Dhairya stated that the team’s priority is “protecting the network and community assets.” He also promised to continue to provide relevant and transparent updates on this case as the investigation continues: “We will continue to provide transparent updates as the investigation progresses.”

data.title: Democrats Name Their Price for Avoiding Government Shutdown — WSJ
data.description:
By Siobhan Hughes, Lindsay Wise and Anna Wilde Mathews

WASHINGTON — The next federal-funding deadline is just weeks away. Democrats say restoring billions of dollars in health-insurance subsidies for Americans is the minimum price of their vote, holding the threat of a shutdown over Republicans' heads.

Democrats are more confident in their negotiating stance compared with March, when a similar attempt to play hardball fizzled. While today's talks pit them against Republican leaders who want a two-month spending deal with no big additions, they are finding support among rank-and-file GOP lawmakers fearing political backlash if their voters are hit by higher health-insurance bills next year.

"Republicans have to come to meet with us in a true bipartisan negotiation to satisfy the American people's needs on healthcare, or they won't get our votes, plain and simple," Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer (D., N.Y.) said Thursday.

Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R., S.D.) said he was open to discussions about extending the subsidies, but not as a condition of a short-term spending bill he has proposed to keep the government open into November. Such an extension would give Congress more time to iron out full-year spending deals.

The fight centers on the extension of expiring Affordable Care Act tax credits. The subsidies, which expanded the payments offered under the existing ACA, or Obamacare, were first passed in 2021 by Democrats as a temporary measure tied to a Covid-19 aid law. Republicans didn't renew them in their sprawling tax-and-spending legislation President Trump signed in July, meaning they will expire at the end of this year.

About 24.3 million Americans are enrolled in Obamacare, with about 22.4 million of them receiving subsidies, according to health researcher KFF. All of those subsidies would shrink or be zeroed out if the additional enhancement ended.

The enhanced federal payments made Obamacare plans far cheaper for lower-income and many middle-class consumers. If the payments aren't extended, millions of ACA insurance enrollees who benefited from the expanded subsidies will see their health-insurance bills soar. KFF estimates that the monthly premium bills paid by those who get subsidies will increase by more than 75% on average, which in turn could prompt many to drop coverage altogether.

In the past, Republicans have opposed both Obamacare — which they unsuccessfully tried to repeal in 2017 — and the extra subsidies. While ending the extra payments would serve the broader GOP goal to cut government spending, it would leave the party vulnerable heading into the 2026 midterms, when Republicans will work to hang on to their narrow control of the House and Senate.

The prospect has some Republican lawmakers worried.

"If we don't do something, some day soon, premiums are going to skyrocket, and not by a little bit," said Sen. Josh Hawley (R., Mo.). "I mean, we're looking at massive increases. People will not be able to afford it."

Sen. Thom Tillis (R., N.C.), who is retiring after this term, said he thought two things drove election outcomes: "One is a bad economy. The other one is bad decisions on healthcare," he said.

Trump's own pollsters have warned of political consequences. A memo published in July by the firm of Trump pollster Tony Fabrizio found overwhelming support in the most competitive House districts for extending the subsidies. Another pollster who has advised Trump on his campaigns, John McLaughlin, raised the alarm in an opinion article in Townhall last month, saying if subsidies expire, Trump voters will get an unexpected tax hike.

In the House, Republicans have a 219-213 majority and could pass the legislation along party lines if nearly all Republicans stick together. In the Senate, Republicans have only a 53-47 majority, shy of the 60 votes needed to pass most legislation, and will need some Democratic support.

House Speaker Mike Johnson (R., La.) raised the possibility of extending the subsidies in an interview in Punchbowl this month though he noted that many conservatives would be staunchly opposed to the idea for cost and policy reasons. The Congressional Budget Office estimates that making the enhanced subsidies permanent would add $335 billion to the deficit over a 10-year period through 2034.

"I don't love the policy, OK?" he said. "But I understand the political realities and the realities of people on the ground."

Rep. Ralph Norman (R., S.C.) said that he couldn't support legislation extending the subsidies "unless we have some offsets — we're going to be battling for that."

Sen. John Cornyn (R., Texas), who is in a competitive primary, said the expiring subsidies "need to be revisited." Cornyn said he backed Thune's decision not to include a subsidies fix in the short-term spending bill Senate Republicans want to pass before the end of the month. He predicted Congress would deal with the expiring subsidies by the end of the year.

Democrats also face heightened risks. The progressive wing wants the party to show it can fight and win after Senate Democrats earlier this year shied away from triggering a government shutdown and then the Trump administration proceeded to selectively withhold congressionally appropriated funds.

Schumer struck a similarly tough tone in March, when he threatened a shutdown over a Republican-written spending bill, which progressives saw as their best chance to stand up to cuts of government jobs and programs. But he backed down after concluding that a shutdown would empower the Trump administration to make even bigger cuts. Progressives said he chickened out.

Trump meanwhile has tried to pour cold water on talk of any deal with Democrats. "If you gave them every dream, they would not vote for it," he said Friday on Fox News.

Some Democrats and activists have argued that the party also should use the shutdown as leverage to restore GOP cuts to Medicaid spending. But the spotlight has remained on ACA payments, in part because of the potential interest among some Republicans.

"It's not the only issue that we're focused on, but it's probably the unifier," said Sen. Tim Kaine (D., Va.). Of Republicans, he said: "It feels like we're pushing on a door...that may be open."

Write to Siobhan Hughes at Siobhan.hughes@wsj.com, Lindsay Wise at lindsay.wise@wsj.com and Anna Wilde Mathews at Anna.Mathews@wsj.com

data.title: Tens of thousands join pro-Palestinian march in New Zealand's biggest city
data.description:
Thousands took part in a pro-Palestinian march in Auckland, New Zealand's biggest city, on Saturday, in what organisers said was the largest rally of its kind since the war in Gaza began between Israel and militant Islamist Palestinian group Hamas.

Some 50,000 attended the March for Humanity rally in central Auckland on Saturday morning, the Aotearoa for Palestine group said. New Zealand police estimated the attendance at 20,000.

Aotearoa for Palestine spokesperson Arama Rata said it was New Zealand's largest march in support of Palestinians since conflict broke out in Gaza in October 2023, when Israel launched an offensive in retaliation of a Hamas-led cross-border attack that killed about 1,200 people and resulted in the capture of 251 hostages.

Palestinian authorities have said that more than 64,000 people have been killed in the conflict in Gaza, while humanitarian organisations say a shortage of food is leading to widespread starvation.

Many in Saturday's protest crowd carried Palestinian flags and banners with slogans including "Don't normalise genocide" and "Grow a spine stand with Palestine", public broadcaster Radio New Zealand reported.

Organisers, motivated by a march that shut down Sydney's iconic Harbour Bridge in August, wanted to close a major city bridge with Saturday's rally, Rata said, but were forced to abandon those plans on Friday due to strong winds.

Police said there were no arrests at the march and that roads along the route were being reopened.

Aotearoa for Palestine said it wanted New Zealand's centre-right coalition government to impose sanctions on Israel.

Prime Minister Christopher Luxon in August described recent Israeli actions in Gaza, including a lack of humanitarian assistance, as "utterly appalling", and New Zealand has been weighing up whether to recognise a Palestinian state.

Ben Kepes, a spokesperson for the New Zealand Jewish Council, a body representing around 10,000 Jews who live in the country, said he appreciated the peaceful nature of Saturday's march but condemned calls to sanction Israel.

"We are concerned sanctions against Israel singles Israel out, fails to recognise the attacks on Israel and ignores the plight of the hostages," Kepes said in a statement.

data.title: RailTel wins multiple orders worth over ₹600 cr from Bihar Education Project Council
data.description:
RailTel Corporation of India Ltd has received a Letter of Acceptance worth ₹209.79 crore from the Bihar Education Project Council for implementing the Education Quality Enhancement initiative under PM SHRI. The project is scheduled for completion by September 2026.

The company said the order involves supply and implementation services aimed at strengthening educational infrastructure across the state.

It will cover ICT labs, Integrated Science and Mathematics labs, smart classrooms, and teaching material supply, enhancing access to modern learning tools for schools across Bihar.

Shares of RailTel closed 2.86% higher at ₹373.80 on Friday, reflecting continued investor interest amid a series of contract wins.

Earlier this week, the company also informed that it had received multiple orders worth around ₹396 crore from BEPC.

On Thursday, RailTel announced it had bagged a ₹70.94 crore contract from Nashik Municipal Smart City Development Corporation Ltd. The project, due by December 2026, involves setting up the Nashik and Trimbakeshwar City Network Backbone, along with operations and maintenance services.

In addition, the company has secured a ₹32.51 crore order from Panvel Municipal Corporation for providing connectivity under the Panvel Safe City Project. This project, slated for completion in March 2031, covers supply, installation, and SD-WAN based internet and MPLS connectivity across multiple locations.

data.title: Galaxy Digital Head Says U.S. Will Announce Strategic Bitcoin Reserve This Year
data.description:
The momentum for a Strategic Bitcoin Reserve in the U.S. is fast becoming reality, backed by hard numbers, new legislation, and concrete progress in both Congress and the Treasury.

Alex Thorn, head of research at Galaxy Digital, says there’s a strong chance the U.S. could create a Strategic Bitcoin Reserve this year.

Here’s how the plan stands as of September 2025.

U.S. To Form a Strategic Bitcoin Reserve This Year

Alex Thorn, suggested that the U.S. government might soon make a historic announcement. According to Thorn, there is a strong chance that officials will reveal the creation of a “Strategic Bitcoin Reserve” (SBR) before the end of this year.

The idea may sound bold, but Thorn says people are underestimating how possible it is.

Alex Thorn
@intangiblecoins

yes, i mean the U.S. government announcing, not bessent’s offhand comment on tv

that comment gave us hints of where they think the size of the reserve stands but is not a formal announcement of the SBR https://t.co/ADxguLJ8vH

Sep 11, 2025

If it happens, it would mean the U.S. sees Bitcoin not just as an investment, but as a national asset, much like its gold reserves.

Bitcoin Treasury Plan Is Progressing

The momentum for a federally-backed Bitcoin reserve began in March 2025 when President Trump signed an executive order directing the Department of the Treasury to consolidate seized Bitcoin and digital assets into a centralized “Strategic Bitcoin Reserve.

The move was designed to treat Bitcoin as a sovereign-grade asset, similar to the gold treasury at Fort Knox or the Strategic Petroleum Reserve, rather than simply auctioning seized coins to the public.

Just this week, the U.S. House advanced Bill H.R. 5166, requiring Treasury to report within 90 days on frameworks for holding and securing federal Bitcoin assets

How Much Bitcoin Could the U.S. Be Holding?

Alex Thorn estimates the U.S. government’s Bitcoin reserve could be worth $15–20 billion, equal to about 120,000–160,000 BTC at today’s prices.

In reality, the U.S. already controls a massive stash. Over the years, agencies have seized coins from the Silk Road, the Bitfinex hack, and other criminal cases. Altogether, that’s about 198,000 BTC, nearly $23 billion, making the U.S. the largest known state holder of Bitcoin.

Skepticism Still Remains

Still, not everyone expects a quick announcement. Dave Weisberger of CoinRoutes says the government may stay quiet until it has quietly built a reserve, with 2026 a more likely date.

But others warn delay could be risky. Samson Mow, founder of Jan3, argues waiting too long could let other countries get ahead. 

While no clear roadmap is out yet, recent moves in Washington suggest momentum is building.

Bitcoin Price Will Skyrocket 

If the U.S. confirms a Strategic Bitcoin Reserve this year, it would be one of the most significant financial policy shifts in decades. This will skyrocket the bitcoin price to nearly $200K.

As of now, Bitcoin price is trading around $115,822 reflecting a jump of 4% in a week.

data.title: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1838 GMT - Adobe's F3Q results mark a turning point, with key growth metrics in its core and new AI-first products trending positively, BofA Securities analysts say in a note. The report should help more investors see Adobe as an AI beneficiary, though a debate over whether it's an AI winner or loser is likely to continue, the analysts say. Going forward, gradual, continued traction with AI-first products will likely drive reaccelerating growth, the analysts say, estimating an incremental 1% point of topline growth in fiscal 2027, assuming it maintains its current quarterly AI net new annual recurring revenue run rate. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1826 GMT - A potential merger between Paramount Skydance and Warner Bros. Discovery makes sense strategically, but a full integration would take time, BofA Securities analysts say in a note. They estimate that integration and restructuring under the combined company would likely take years to implement, pointing to the lengthy process that came with Warner Bros.' combination with Discovery. That said, the deal would significantly boost Paramount's prospects, particularly in film and television, given access to Warner Bros.' best-in-class library, they say. It would also create a formidable competitor in streaming with the potential combination of Paramount+ and HBO Max, they say. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1819 GMT - 2025 will mark the end of one of Bank of America's biggest investment themes for this decade: the trend of moving away from Chinese assets known as the "Anywhere But China" trade, the bank's analysts say in a research note. Amid the AI bubble in the U.S., investors should take a "barbell" approach to investing, with capital flowing to a mix of low-risk and high-risk global technology investments, the analysts say. China and Japan are bright spots in this new paradigm, they say. Chinese technology companies are an optimal way to barbell the U.S. AI bubble, the analysts say. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1521 GMT - A potential merger between Paramount Skydance and Warner Bros. Discovery would likely clear regulatory scrutiny, according to Benchmark. The timing is "particularly auspicious" says analyst Daniel Kurnos. He says the FCC's September preliminary agenda seeks to potentially eliminate the rule prohibiting dual ownership of broadcast networks. While CBS is the only broadcast network and station group involved in this potential deal, it "suggests an openness to further media consolidation of the major players," Kurnos says. The Ellison family's close family ties with the Trump Administration will likely also help avoid potential obstacles to the merger, Benchmark says. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1440 GMT - A Paramount Skydance/Warner Bros. Discovery merger "could yield meaningful upside for the combined entity," says Benchmark analyst Daniel Kurnos. Two years ago, Benchmark estimated a merger between the two companies would create synergies between $3.15 billion and $4.9 billion, although the companies have since begun their own cost reduction programs. "The math remains relatively the same, and the industrial logic has only gotten stronger in what appears to be a consolidating media landscape," Kurnos says. The analyst adds that even without synergies, "Paramount could pay $18 per share for Warner and still have the deal be value neutral or possibly even marginally accretive." Warner Bros. Discovery surges 12%, while Paramount Skydance is up 4%. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1320 GMT - A Paramount Skydance merger with Warner Bros. Discovery could get more difficult if Paramount doesn't act quickly, says Benchmark analyst Matthew Harrigan. The Warner Bros. Discovery separation is planned for 2Q26, which would make a Paramount bid for the company tax challenged for 12 months, "providing an impetus for a serious near-term offer," Harrigan says. The separate Warner Bros. and Discovery Global stocks could also get more expensive as Harrigan says investors are currently undervaluing the company's earnings potential with the Studio and HBO Max divisions set for renewed growth in the wake of an eventual $5 billion in cost reductions. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1307 GMT - Sony, Netflix, Apple and Amazon could all be potential bidders for parts of Warner Bros. Discovery, but another suitor is unlikely to challenge Paramount Skydance's acquisition bid, says Benchmark analyst Matthew Harrigan. Paramount Skydance has privileged access to capital and could create more synergies with a merger than other potential bidders. The company is also best positioned to manage the decline in linear television networks while transitioning to streaming distribution. Sony is unlikely to challenge an Ellison family bid, Netflix is wary of old media influences and Apple and Amazon could be turned off by share price impact, Harrigan says. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1225 GMT - Mizuho analysts say in a research note that they expect Applied Materials to lose market share. The semiconductor-equipment maker's revenue is largely exposed to segments that are facing increased competition from China, the analysts say. As a result, Applied Materials is losing customers. This comes after the company last month forecast a drop in profit and revenue this quarter, hurt by challenges in China and choppy demand across end markets. Mizuho downgrades Applied Materials to neutral and lowers its price target to $175 from $200. Applied Materials is off 2% premarket. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1159 GMT - Adobe logs better-than-expected F3Q results, with its small-but-quickly growing AI-infused product portfolio hitting its annual recurring revenue goal a quarter early, KeyBanc says. "The outlook for next quarter is moving higher, but we are not yet certain the green-shoots are there for a full-fledged, material acceleration in FY26 at this point," analysts Jackson Ader and Jack Nichols say in a research note. While there are many AI model builders developing their own potentially competitive tools according to the analysts, they say Adobe nevertheless remains the center of gravity for the creative process. Adobe shares gain 3% premarket. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1059 GMT - Adobe is fighting an uphill battle, attempting to prove to investors that AI isn't a threat to its core value proposition. The software company did so in its latest quarter, Davidson analysts say in a research note, as it outperformed Wall Street expectations and effectively drove adoption of its expanding AI-infused product portfolio. This momentum around usage and adoption supports a boost to its outlook, they add. "The company also continues to actively buy back shares at current levels and deliver mid-40% operating margins, supporting double digits earnings growth," the analysts write. Davidson maintains its buy rating, and Adobe shares climb 3.8% premarket. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1056 GMT - Adobe's beat-and-raise in its fiscal 3Q doesn't ease long-term concerns, Melius Research analysts say in a note. "The story at the end of the day is revenue growth could decelerate next year," they write, adding that the software company's 4Q guide calls for an on-year slowdown in sales despite price hikes. Adobe benefited from higher prices in its latest quarter, but pricing doesn't seem sustainable given increasing competition. Management notes that competition from Figma, Canva and bigger technology companies such as Meta Platforms doesn't seem to be affecting the lower-end of the market yet. Melius maintains its sell rating on the stock. Adobe shares rise 3.6%. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1406 GMT - Roughly half--48%--of newly built apartments completed in 1Q were rented within three months, according to Redfin. That is up from 47% in 4Q 2024 and 46% the quarter before that. While the recent uptick in the rental absorption rate is small, it's notable because for much of 2021-2023, the absorption rate was declining. It's likely inching up now because the number of new apartments hitting the market is falling. Renters have fewer options to choose from, making it easier for landlords to fill units fast. As many Americans find themselves priced out of homeownership, rents are beginning to rise again. Apartment building permits have fallen below pre-pandemic levels. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

1329 GMT - The current offer put forward by BBVA is sufficiently attractive for Sabadell shareholders to accept it, RBC Capital Markets says in a research note. BBVA, which last year launched a hostile bid for its smaller rival, opened the acceptance period to Sabadell shareholders earlier this week. Sabadell's board has recommended that shareholders don't tender their shares. "Both banks are arriving at very different answers and, in reality, the answer is probably somewhere in the middle," analyst Benjamin Toms writes. There is potential to create value out of a deal even if it isn't as large or quickly realizable as BBVA estimates, he notes. To avoid being left with an acceptance rate between 30% and 50%, BBVA could still boost the price of the offer by around 10%, he calculates. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

1229 GMT - Italian banks are in a good place with loan and fee growth picking up and consolidation scenarios still to play out, Keefe, Bruyette & Woods say in a research note. "The Italian banks continue to live some of their best lives, with strong [returns on tangible equity] and high payout ratios, but also dynamic management teams that continue to work hard to improve their businesses," analysts Hugo Cruz and Ben Maher write after a trip to Milan. They point to more evidence of a pick up in corporate loan growth and stable or easing loan pricing competition, supporting net interest income trends. The market might need more clarity on the outcome of Monte dei Paschi's Mediobanca takeover before another potential M&A round, they add. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

1024 GMT - The current price that BBVA is offering for Sabadell is insufficient, says a Sabadell board member that represents its third largest shareholder. Fintech Advisory holds a 3.86% stake in the Spanish bank. "In my opinion, the transaction presented by BBVA is the right strategy for both institutions, although at a price that currently makes it unfeasible," David Martinez Guzman says. He adds that he agrees with the board's rejection of the offer and won't tender the shares he owns or those he manages through the fund. BBVA should reconsider and submit a competitive offer that will allow it to get at least 50% of Sabadell shareholders on board, he says. Sabadell issued its formal opinion on the bid and is recommending that shareholders don't tender their shares to the $18 billion takeover offer. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0915 GMT - The Indonesian government's plan to inject IDR200 trillion into state-owned banks may ease investor concerns over liquidity conditions and rising fund costs, says Nomura analyst Tushar Mohata. The cash injection would boost sector-wide excess liquidity, meaning deposits minus loans, by 37%. That would be good for major lenders, whose shares have been under persistent selling pressure, with valuations well below historical averages, Mohata adds. Nomura thinks SOE banks, particularly Bank Rakyat Indonesia, Bank Mandiri and Bank Negara Indonesia are well-positioned to deploy new liquidity into higher-margin segments, while Bank Syariah Indonesia needs more funding discipline. It retains buy calls on all four. (jason.chau@wsj.com)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1923 GMT - Oil futures post weekly gains with geopolitical risk premium outweighing oversupply concerns as the Russia-Ukraine war continues unabated and an Israeli strike aimed at Hamas leaders in Qatar threatens to increase tensions in the Middle East. The market took in stride OPEC+ plans to continue unwinding output cuts in October, while another unexpected weekly U.S. crude stock build was seen as bearish. Additional U.K. sanctions against Russia and U.S. calls for Europe to hit India and China with tariffs for buying Russian oil gave prices a lift at the end of the week. WTI settles up 0.5% at $62.69 for a 1.3% weekly gain, and Brent rises 0.9% to $66.99, up 2.3% from a week ago. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1916 GMT - Oil futures post weekly gains with geopolitical risk premium outweighing oversupply concerns as the Russia-Ukraine war continues unabated and an Israeli strike aimed at Hamas leaders in Qatar threatens to increase tensions in the Middle East. The market took in stride OPEC+ plans to continue unwinding output cuts in October, while another unexpected weekly U.S. crude stock build was seen as bearish. Additional U.K. sanctions against Russia and U.S. calls for Europe to hit India and China with tariffs for buying Russian oil gave prices a lift at the end of the week. WTI settles up 0.5% at $62.69 for a 1.3% weekly gain, and Brent rises 0.9% to $66.99, up 2.3% from a week ago. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1833 GMT - The number of rigs drilling for oil in the U.S. rose for a third consecutive week, increasing by two to 416, or 72 fewer than a year ago, Baker Hughes reports. Rigs directed at natural gas were unchanged this week at 118, up by 21 from a year earlier. The EIA expects drilling activity in 2026 to be more centered in natural gas-intensive regions given its forecasts for lower oil but higher natural gas prices. "We forecast that crude oil will trade at its lowest premium to natural gas since 2005," the EIA said in its latest Short Term Energy Outlook. "We expect decreases in natural gas produced as a by-product of oil directed drilling will offset increases in that produced by natural gas-directed drilling." (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1433 GMT - Oil futures are recovering from the previous day's losses as the U.K. announces new sanctions on Russia and the U.S. urges Europe to put tariff pressure on buyers of Russian oil, namely India and China. "The Russia/Ukraine war remains a positive to crude prices with Ukraine hitting a major oil export facility in Russia late yesterday," Dennis Kissler of BOK Financial says in a note. "Russia exports nearly 7 million barrels a day, therefore any disruptions whether from tariffs or war conflicts could be a bullish catalyst for crude prices near-term." WTI is up 1.5% at $63.28 a barrel and Brent is up 1.6% at $67.43. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1308 GMT - Equinor faces challenges from an expected decline in European gas prices and lower financial flexibility due to its participation in Orsted's rights issue, UBS analysts write. European gas storage has refilled steadily, while at the same time new LNG projects have started up. Longer term, LNG investments are picking up and UBS expects gas prices to drop even further than previously. Meanwhile, the Danish wind-farm developer has confirmed it will participate in the Orsted rights issue to maintain its 10% stake, at a cost of $939 million. "This will further increase gearing and reduce financial flexibility, increasing risks around shareholder returns into next year." UBS cuts its price target on the stock to 210 kroner ($21) from 220 kroner and reiterates its sell rating. Shares rise 0.5% to 242.50 kroner. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1914 GMT - Rivian's recent recall adds another stumbling block for the electric carmaker. The company is recalling some 24,214 of its R1S and R1T electric vehicles due to a software defect that may cause its Highway Assist system to misclassify other vehicles, the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration says. The recall comes as Rivian faces a number of hurdles, including the end of a $7,500 federal tax credit for EVs that expires at the end of the month. The company has also laid off workers as it comes under pressure to lower costs as it prepares for the rollout of its next model. Shares fall 3.5%, to $13.48. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1900 GMT - Airlines are experiencing stronger demand in the fall rather than the summer, Deutsche analyst Michel Linenberg says. October looks better than September and September looks better than August, he says, citing booking trends and commentary from executives at a conference this week show. Strong premium demand has been a significant driver of this trend, as well as bounce-back in corporate travel after a slow period this spring. Other segments, such as the main cabin and domestic markets, remain sluggish. Airlines that depend on main cabin domestic sales are restructuring their networks, Linenberg says. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1611 GMT - Amazon Autos hasn't hit top gear yet, especially in used vehicles, say Davidson analysts Tom White and Wyatt Swanson. Amazon Autos was launched in 2023 with a partnership with Hyundai for new vehicles, but the analysts say that Hyundai dealers are still seeing very limited sales through Amazon. Early last month, Amazon said dealers would be able to list their used vehicles on Amazon Autos, but " there remains relatively scant Used inventory available on the Amazon Autos platform," the analysts say. Almost all of the used inventory that is available is from Hertz, which recently said it was partnering with Amazon, and participating Hyundai dealers, the analysts say. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1526 GMT - Tariffs were expected to hit the auto industry hard, "yet six months into the onset of tariffs, we've been positively surprised by the extent to which the industry has held in better than anticipated," Barclays analysts Dan Levy and Josh Cho say in a research note, citing minimal disruption to production and sales, as well as ongoing consumer strength. Barclays upgrades its sector view on the U.S. autos and mobility to neutral from negative, as tariffs are now well understood by the market and likely to ease with additional trade deals. General Motors and Aptiv are especially well positioned, the analysts note, upgrading each to overweight from equal weight. GM should benefit from easing electric-vehicle regulations and resiliency in pricing, while parts supplier Aptiv can benefit from improved business dynamics alongside reasonable valuation, the analysts say. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1257 GMT - Mercedes-Benz could see some EBIT improvement by 2027, but shares have catalysts for improvement in 2026, Citi analysts write. EBIT margins have fallen by around 1,000 basis points on a combination of China market challenges, volume and pricing declines, U.S. tariffs, and the poor performance of previous EV investments. The company is now responding by cutting fixed and variable costs and a new S class model cycle should drive some on-year improvement in 2027, Citi adds. "We think Mercedes should commit to a fixed rather than a free cash flow-based annual return and restart the buyback to signal long-term confidence." Shares fall 1.5%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

1233 GMT - BMW said this week that some of its assumptions regarding second-half earnings had changed, which Citi analysts say will focus investor minds. A delay in signing the EU-U.S. tariff agreement means manufacturers are paying 27.5% rather than 15% on imports for longer--potentially costing BMW around 200 million euros in third-quarter EBIT, the bank estimates. More worrying is that BMW's China assumptions have again proven too optimistic, resulting in a sharper China sales decline in 2025 and dealer compensation payments hitting margins, Citi says. Despite cost cuts and a better performance in other regions, "the worry remains that BMW is no longer competitive in China and the deterioration continues to weigh on earnings going forward." Shares fall 1.5%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

1203 GMT - Transat A.T. faces a number of headwinds in the near-term, but TD Cowen's Tim James says in a note that he doesn't believe they should shift investor focus from attractive valuation when looking out over the next 12 months. The analyst says drags related to demand softness, increased competition and supply were themes in the F3Q report, but the airline's Elevation cost and revenue enhancement strategy, debt reduction, partnership with Porter airlines, along with improving demand "should allow Transat to generate structurally higher earnings despite short-term headwinds." (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

0820 GMT - China Eastern Airlines' outlook could remain challenging, says DBS Group Research's Paul Yong in a note. He expects the airline's profitability to remain under strain as passenger yield pressures endure, driven by fare wars, muted business travel demand and fiercer competition from China's growing high-speed rail network. He lowers his 2025 and 2026 earnings forecasts for the airline by 47% and 52%, respectively. DBS trims its target on China Eastern's A shares to CNY4.10 from CNY4.30, and on its H shares to HK$2.90 from HK$3.10, while maintaining its hold rating. China Eastern's Shanghai-listed shares closed 1.2% lower at CNY4.03, while its Hong Kong-listed stock ended flat at HK$2.97. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

data.title: Turkey orders detention of Istanbul district mayor, others in corruption probe, state media says
data.description:
A Turkish prosecutor ordered the detention of 48 suspects, including the mayor of Istanbul's opposition-run Bayrampasa district, as part of a corruption investigation, state broadcaster TRT Haber said on Saturday.

The police carried out early morning raids at 72 locations to seize documents and detain suspects on charges including embezzlement, bribery, and tender rigging, according to TRT Haber.

In a post on X, Bayrampasa Mayor Hasan Mutlu, from the main opposition Republican People's Party (CHP), said he had nothing to hide and called the investigation "a political operation based on unfounded slander".

The detentions come amidst a nearly year-long crackdown on the CHP and CHP-run municipalities, in which hundreds of party members have been arrested and jailed.

A court ruling due on Monday could remove the leader of the CHP in a case widely seen as a test of the country’s fragile balance between democratic institutions and centralised power, increasing the legal pressure on the party.

data.title: Analyst Sets Bold $1,314 Target For Solana After Cup-And-Handle Breakout
data.description:
A cryptocurrency analyst has pointed out how Solana may be moving toward a sky-high main target based on a cup-and-handle breakout.

Solana Is Breaking Out Of A Cup And Handle

In a new post on X, analyst Ali Martinez has shared a pattern that the 1-week price of SOL has appeared to have been following during the last few years. The pattern in question is a “cup and handle” from technical analysis (TA).

It’s made up of two parts: a U-shaped trendline forming the “cup” and a downward parallel channel representing the “handle.” As the asset moves through the pattern, it first goes through a decline to a low, then observes a rebound back to about the same height as before the drawdown. Finally, it once again witnesses a bearish trajectory, this time one consistent with a descending channel.

When the price is inside the channel, the usual rules of the formation apply: the upper line acts as a resistance barrier, while the lower one provides support. A break out of the levels can imply a continuation of trend in that direction.

A cup and handle is generally assumed to be a bullish continuation pattern, meaning that breakouts can be considered more probable to occur in the up direction.

The same seems to have held true for Solana as well, as its 1-week price has just seen a surge beyond the resistance line of its multi-year cup and handle pattern.

As displayed in the above graph, the weekly Solana price went down and up along a cup between 2021 and 2024, but this year, it entered into the handle or downward consolidation phase.

After some period of stay inside the zone, the coin finally found a break away from it in July. This escape from the descending channel occurred with an upward surge, a potential sign that sustained bullish momentum may be coming for SOL, if the pattern is anything to go by.

As for what could be a potential target for the cryptocurrency’s price, the analyst has suggested a rather bold one: $1,314.41. The target corresponds to a line from Fibonacci Extension levels.

Fibonacci Extension levels are drawn on an asset’s price chart starting from a bottom point. Martinez has taken SOL’s cup low as the zero level. Each successive level corresponds to some percentage extension from this bottom, equal to a ratio from the popular Fibonacci series.

The 1.414 Fibonacci Extension level lies at $1,314.41 for Solana right now. It now remains to be seen whether the coin will eventually surge to this mark, or if the breakout will fizzle out.

SOL Price

Solana has performed better than most top coins over the past week as its price has surged by more than 17%, reaching the $238 mark.

data.title: Adani Power wins 2,400 MW thermal power project in Bihar’s Bhagalpur.
data.description:
One of the country’s largest private sector thermal power generator, https://www.cnbctv18.com/market/stocks/adani-power-share-price/AP11/Adani Power Ltd. has received a Letter of Intent from Bihar State Power Generation Company Ltd for setting up a 2,400 megawatt plant in Pirpainti, Bhagalpur.

The company has signed a 25-year power supply agreement with the state utility for electricity generated from the upcoming greenfield ultra supercritical thermal power plant.

The project, designed on a build, finance, own, and operate basis, will consist of three units of 800 MW each.

The project was awarded following a competitive bidding process in which Adani Power emerged successful by offering the lowest supply rate at ₹6.075 per kilowatt hour. The company plans to invest nearly $3 billion in the plant and its associated infrastructure.

The coal requirement for the facility has been tied up under the Government of India’s SHAKTI policy. According to the company, the project is expected to generate 10,000–12,000 direct and indirect jobs during the construction phase, and around 3,000 jobs once operational.

The full commissioning of the project is targeted within 60 months.

This agreement builds on the Letter of Award issued in August by Bihar State Power Generation Company on behalf of North and South Bihar power distribution companies.

On Friday, shares of Adani Power closed 3.86% higher at ₹648.75 on the NSE.

data.title: London police brace for large rival protests
data.description:
Key points:
  • 'Unite the Kingdom' march organised by anti-Islam activists
  • Counter protest organised by 'Stand Up To Racism'
  • 1,000 police officers deployed for protests

London police will deploy extra officers on Saturday as right-wing, anti-immigrant protesters and opposing anti-racism demonstrators stage rival rallies, raising concerns over potential clashes.

A "Unite the Kingdom" march organised by anti-immigrant and anti-Muslim activist Stephen Yaxley-Lennon, also known as Tommy Robinson, is due to meet near London's Waterloo Bridge before making its way to the southern end of Whitehall where a rally will take place.

A counter-protest, organised by "Stand Up To Racism", is due to gather at the other end of Whitehall, the main thoroughfare towards parliament.

The Unite the Kingdom march is expected to mourn Charlie Kirk, the American conservative activist shot dead on Wednesday as he gave a talk at a university in Utah.

London's Metropolitan Police said barriers will be in place to create space between the two groups.

It said more than 1,600 officers will be deployed across London on Saturday, including 500 brought in from other forces, as in addition to the marches there are high-profile soccer fixtures including West Ham versus Tottenham, concerts and other events.

Around 1,000 officers will be on duty at the marches.

"We will approach them as we do any other protests, policing without fear or favour, ensuring people can exercise their lawful rights but being robust in dealing with incidents or offences should they occur," said Commander Clair Haynes, who is leading the policing operation.

Last Saturday, nearly 900 people were arrested at a London demonstration against the ban on the group Palestine Action.

Immigration has become the dominant political issue in Britain, eclipsing concerns over a faltering economy, as the country faces a record number of asylum claims and arrivals by migrants in small boats across the Channel, including more than 28,000 this year.

Facing mounting public criticism over immigration, Prime Minister Keir Starmer last week appointed Shabana Mahmood as Britain's new interior minister in a shake-up of his government.
